***Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2*** is a 2017 American [superhero film](/wiki/Superhero_film "Superhero film") based on the [Marvel Comics](/wiki/Marvel_Comics "Marvel Comics") superhero team [Guardians of the Galaxy](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(2008_team) "Guardians of the Galaxy (2008 team)"), produced by [Marvel Studios](/wiki/Marvel_Studios "Marvel Studios") and distributed by [Walt Disney Studios Motion Pictures](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_Motion_Pictures "Walt Disney Studios Motion Pictures"). It is the sequel to *[Guardians of the Galaxy](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film) "Guardians of the Galaxy (film)")* (2014) and [the 15th film](/wiki/List_of_Marvel_Cinematic_Universe_films "List of Marvel Cinematic Universe films") in the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") (MCU). Written and directed by [James Gunn](/wiki/James_Gunn "James Gunn"), the film stars an [ensemble cast](/wiki/Ensemble_cast "Ensemble cast") featuring [Chris Pratt](/wiki/Chris_Pratt "Chris Pratt"), [Zoe Saldaña](/wiki/Zoe_Salda%C3%B1a "Zoe Saldaña"), [Dave Bautista](/wiki/Dave_Bautista "Dave Bautista"), [Vin Diesel](/wiki/Vin_Diesel "Vin Diesel"), [Bradley Cooper](/wiki/Bradley_Cooper "Bradley Cooper"), [Michael Rooker](/wiki/Michael_Rooker "Michael Rooker"), [Karen Gillan](/wiki/Karen_Gillan "Karen Gillan"), [Pom Klementieff](/wiki/Pom_Klementieff "Pom Klementieff"), [Sylvester Stallone](/wiki/Sylvester_Stallone "Sylvester Stallone"), and [Kurt Russell](/wiki/Kurt_Russell "Kurt Russell"). In the film, the [Guardians](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Guardians of the Galaxy (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") travel throughout the cosmos as they help [Peter Quill](/wiki/Peter_Quill_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Peter Quill (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") (Pratt) learn more about his mysterious parentage.

The film was officially announced at the 2014 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con") before the theatrical release of the first film, along with Gunn's return as writer and director from the first film, with the title of the sequel revealed a year later in June 2015. [Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") began in February 2016 at [Pinewood Atlanta Studios](/wiki/Pinewood_Atlanta_Studios "Pinewood Atlanta Studios") in [Fayette County, Georgia](/wiki/Fayette_County,_Georgia "Fayette County, Georgia"), with many crew changes from the first film due to other commitments. Filming concluded in June 2016. Gunn chose to set the sequel shortly after the first film to explore the characters' new roles as the Guardians, and to follow the storyline of Quill's father established throughout that previous film. Russell was confirmed as Quill's father in July 2016, portraying Ego, a departure from Quill's comic father.

*Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2* premiered in Tokyo on April 10, 2017, and was released in the United States on May 5, 2017, as part of [Phase Three](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_Three "Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase Three") of the MCU. It grossed more than $869 million worldwide, making it the [eighth-highest-grossing film of 2017](/wiki/2017_in_film#Highest-grossing_films "2017 in film"), while also outgrossing its predecessor. The film received praise for its visuals, direction, soundtrack, action sequences, humor, and performances, though some critics deemed it inferior to the original. It received a nomination for [Best Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects") at the [90th Academy Awards](/wiki/90th_Academy_Awards "90th Academy Awards"). A sequel, *[Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._3 "Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3")*, was released on May 5, 2023, preceded by the [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") special *[The Guardians of the Galaxy Holiday Special](/wiki/The_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Holiday_Special "The Guardians of the Galaxy Holiday Special")* in November 2022.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

In 2014, [Peter Quill](/wiki/Peter_Quill_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Peter Quill (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), [Gamora](/wiki/Gamora_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Gamora (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), [Drax](/wiki/Drax_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Drax (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), [Rocket](/wiki/Rocket_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Rocket (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), and [Baby Groot](/wiki/Baby_Groot_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Baby Groot (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") are renowned as the [Guardians of the Galaxy](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Guardians of the Galaxy (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"). [High Priestess Ayesha](/wiki/Ayesha_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Ayesha (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") of the [Sovereign](/wiki/Sovereign_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Sovereign (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") race has the Guardians protect valuable Anulax batteries from an Abilisk, an inter-dimensional monster, in exchange for [Nebula](/wiki/Nebula_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Nebula (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), Gamora's estranged sister, who was caught attempting to steal the batteries. After Rocket steals the batteries for himself, the Sovereign attacks the Guardians' ship with a fleet of drones. A mysterious figure destroys the drones and the Guardians crash-land on nearby planet Berhert. The figure reveals himself as [Ego](/wiki/Ego_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Ego (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), Quill's father, and he introduces [Mantis](/wiki/Mantis_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Mantis (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), his naïve [empath](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/empath "wikt:empath") servant. Ego invites Quill, Gamora, and Drax to his home planet, while Rocket and Groot remain behind to repair the ship and watch Nebula.

Ayesha hires [Yondu Udonta](/wiki/Yondu_Udonta_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Yondu Udonta (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") and his crew, who have been exiled from the greater [Ravager](/wiki/Ravagers_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Ravagers (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") community by Ravager leader [Stakar Ogord](/wiki/Stakar_Ogord_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Stakar Ogord (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") for [child trafficking](/wiki/Trafficking_of_children "Trafficking of children"), to recapture the Guardians. They capture Rocket, but when Yondu hesitates to turn over Quill, whom he raised, his lieutenant [Kraglin Obfonteri](/wiki/Kraglin_Obfonteri "Kraglin Obfonteri") questions his objectivity, and [Taserface](/wiki/Taserface_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Taserface (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), another lieutenant, leads a mutiny, aided by Nebula, who shoots Yondu. Taserface executes Yondu's loyalists, and imprisons him and Rocket aboard Yondu's ship. Nebula leaves to find and kill Gamora, whom she blames for the torture inflicted on her by [Thanos](/wiki/Thanos_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Thanos (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), their adoptive father. Kraglin, who is remorseful and never signed up for a mutiny, helps Groot free Rocket and Yondu, and while escaping, the group set the ship to self-destruct using Yondu's arrow. Taserface informs the Sovereign before being killed by the explosion.

Ego, a god-like [Celestial](/wiki/Celestials_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Celestials (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") that manipulated the matter around his consciousness to form his "home" planet, explains that he projected a humanoid guise to travel the universe and discover a purpose, eventually falling in love with Quill's mother [Meredith](/wiki/Meredith_Quill_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Meredith Quill (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"). Ego hired Yondu to collect the young Quill after Meredith's death, but the boy was never delivered, and Ego has been searching for him ever since. He teaches Quill to manipulate his inherited Celestial power, while Mantis grows close to Drax and tries to warn him of Ego's plans. Nebula arrives at Ego's planet and tries to kill Gamora, but the pair reconcile and reach an uneasy alliance, soon discovering a cavern filled with skeletal remains. Ego [brainwashes](/wiki/Brainwashing "Brainwashing") Quill and reveals that in his travels, he planted seedlings on thousands of worlds that can [xenoform](/wiki/Terraforming#In_popular_culture "Terraforming") them into new extensions of himself, but only the power of two Celestials can activate them. He adds that he impregnated countless women and hired Yondu to collect their offspring but killed them all when they failed to inherit his Celestial power. When Ego admits that he gave Meredith the brain tumor that killed her, an infuriated Quill breaks out of his brainwashing and violently attacks him. Ego then parasitically draws Quill's energy to activate the seedlings, which begin to consume the worlds on which they are planted.

Rocket, Yondu, Groot, and Kraglin arrive, and together with Mantis, Drax, Nebula and Gamora, they rescue Quill. The reunited Guardians travel to Ego's brain at the planet's core, where Yondu reveals that he kept Quill to spare him from Ego. As the Sovereign's drones return and attack, Rocket makes a bomb using the stolen batteries, which Groot plants on Ego's brain. Quill uses his newfound Celestial powers to fight Ego, distracting him long enough for the other Guardians and Mantis to escape. The bomb explodes, killing Ego and disintegrating the planet, but also draining Quill of his Celestial powers. Yondu sacrifices himself to save Quill by letting himself die in the vacuum of space. Despite having reconciled with Gamora, Nebula chooses to leave and resume her quest to kill Thanos. The Guardians, with Mantis as a new member, hold a funeral for Yondu as dozens of Ravager ships arrive, having heard of Yondu's sacrifice and accept him as a Ravager again.

In a series of mid-and [post-credit scenes](/wiki/Post-credit_scene "Post-credit scene"), Kraglin takes up Yondu's telekinetic arrow and control-fin; Ogord reunites with his ex-teammates and fellow Ravager captains; Ayesha creates [Adam](/wiki/Adam_Warlock_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Adam Warlock (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), a new artificial being with whom she plans to destroy the Guardians;[[a]](#cite_note-7) and Groot grows into a teenager.[[b]](#cite_note-9)

## Cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

From left to right: [Rooker](/wiki/Michael_Rooker "Michael Rooker"), [Pratt](/wiki/Chris_Pratt "Chris Pratt"), [Saldaña](/wiki/Zoe_Salda%C3%B1a "Zoe Saldaña"), [Gillan](/wiki/Karen_Gillan "Karen Gillan"), [Klementieff](/wiki/Pom_Klementieff "Pom Klementieff"), [Bautista](/wiki/Dave_Bautista "Dave Bautista"), [Debicki](/wiki/Elizabeth_Debicki "Elizabeth Debicki"), and [Russell](/wiki/Kurt_Russell "Kurt Russell") promoting the film at the 2016 [San Diego Comic Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic_Con "San Diego Comic Con")

* [Chris Pratt](/wiki/Chris_Pratt "Chris Pratt") as [Peter Quill / Star-Lord](/wiki/Peter_Quill_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Peter Quill (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  The half-human, half-[Celestial](/wiki/Celestial_(comics) "Celestial (comics)") leader of the [Guardians of the Galaxy](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Guardians of the Galaxy (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") who was abducted from Earth as a child and raised by a group of alien thieves and smugglers called the [Ravagers](/wiki/Ravagers_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Ravagers (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[8]](#cite_note-PrattReturn-10)[[9]](#cite_note-ScreenRantJune2015-11) Pratt, who returns as part of a multi-film contract with Marvel,[[10]](#cite_note-HuffingtonPostNov2013-12) said Quill is now famous throughout "the galaxy for having saved so many people... He feels like he's part of this group, a leader of this group. He's a little more responsible and trying to stay out of trouble, but not necessarily doing the best job."[[11]](#cite_note-SDCC2016SlashFilm-13) Pratt stated that working on the film forced him to come to terms with the death of his own father.[[12]](#cite_note-GQNov2016-14) [Wyatt Oleff](/wiki/Wyatt_Oleff "Wyatt Oleff") once again portrays a young Quill.[[13]](#cite_note-ScreenRantCameos-15)
* [Zoe Saldaña](/wiki/Zoe_Salda%C3%B1a "Zoe Saldaña") as [Gamora](/wiki/Gamora_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Gamora (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A member of the Guardians and an orphan from an alien world who seeks redemption for her past crimes. She was trained by Thanos to be his personal assassin.[[9]](#cite_note-ScreenRantJune2015-11) Saldaña described Gamora's role in the team as "the voice of reason", saying, "She's surrounded by all these dudes who are so stupid half the time,"[[14]](#cite_note-USATodayFeb2017-16) and added that she is the "Mom" of the team, saying that she is "just a meticulous, detailed, professional individual."[[15]](#cite_note-ColliderFeb2017-17) Regarding Gamora's relationship with Nebula, Saldaña described it as "volatile" and added, "we're starting somewhere very crazy but appropriate given where we had ended things off in the first installment".[[16]](#cite_note-NebulaGuardian-18)
* [Dave Bautista](/wiki/Dave_Bautista "Dave Bautista") as [Drax the Destroyer](/wiki/Drax_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Drax (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A member of the Guardians and highly skilled warrior.[[9]](#cite_note-ScreenRantJune2015-11) Bautista waited for the final version of the script to not take "away from the magic", which he felt had happened when he read early drafts of the first film.[[17]](#cite_note-BautistaScript-19) He added that "I wasn't crazy about my part [in *Vol. 2*, initially]. It went a different direction than what I thought they were going to go with Drax," noting he did not "think Drax was that significant in the film". The part "clicked" for Bautista after the [table read](/wiki/Read-through "Read-through") with the other cast.[[18]](#cite_note-BautistaCBR-20) Bautista called Drax "more funny, driven" than in the first film,[[19]](#cite_note-BautistaColliderJan2017-21) and having "a sense of innocence and heartbreak about him",[[14]](#cite_note-USATodayFeb2017-16) despite "most people's first perception of Drax [that] he's just a big, muscly brute".[[19]](#cite_note-BautistaColliderJan2017-21) Bautista's makeup took only 90 minutes to apply, down from four hours for the first film. He would have to sit in a sauna at the end of the day to get the makeup off, after his makeup test was found to be too "abrasive".[[15]](#cite_note-ColliderFeb2017-17)
* [Vin Diesel](/wiki/Vin_Diesel "Vin Diesel") as [Baby Groot](/wiki/Groot_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Groot (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A member of the Guardians who is a tree-like humanoid and the accomplice of Rocket.[[20]](#cite_note-DieselReturn-22) The character began growing from a [sapling](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sapling "wikt:sapling") at the end of the first film, with [James Gunn](/wiki/James_Gunn "James Gunn") intending for him to be fully grown by the sequel. Gunn eventually decided to keep him as "Baby Groot", which was one of the reasons the film is set only a few months after the first.[[21]](#cite_note-GunnSet-23) Gunn described Baby Groot as the son of Groot from the first film,[[22]](#cite_note-BabyGrootSon-24) with Diesel explaining that "we're going to see this goofy, adorable, baby Groot [just] kinda learning as he goes."[[23]](#cite_note-GunnGrootNoMemories-25)[[24]](#cite_note-DieselNov2016-26) [Prop master](/wiki/Property_master "Property master") Russell Bobbitt created a 1:1 scale model of the 10-inch (25 cm) Baby Groot for filming, to use as a lighting reference and sometimes as a puppet for the actors to interact with.[[25]](#cite_note-SlashFilmFeb2017Pg3-27) As Groot only communicates with the phrase "I am Groot" in different inflections, Gunn created a "Groot Version" of the script for himself and Diesel, which contains each of Groot's lines in English.[[26]](#cite_note-GrootScript-28) Diesel used a higher register of his voice for Baby Groot, which was pitched up by seven to nine [semitones](/wiki/Semitone "Semitone") depending on the take. He also delivered lines slowly to avoid any time stretching issues.[[27]](#cite_note-DieselPitchShift-29) Diesel recorded Groot's voice for sixteen foreign-language releases of the film (up from six in the first film).[[28]](#cite_note-DieselForeignLanguage-30) [Sean Gunn](/wiki/Sean_Gunn "Sean Gunn") provided on-set reference for adolescent Groot in the post-credit sequence.[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31)
* [Bradley Cooper](/wiki/Bradley_Cooper "Bradley Cooper") as [Rocket](/wiki/Rocket_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Rocket (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A member of the Guardians who is a genetically engineered [raccoon](/wiki/Raccoon "Raccoon")-based bounty hunter, mercenary, and master of weapons and battle tactics.[[9]](#cite_note-ScreenRantJune2015-11) Sean Gunn once again served as the stand-in for the character during filming,[[30]](#cite_note-SGunnRocket-32) with Cooper's performance also referenced.[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31) Sean Gunn said that "Rocket has the same sort of crisis of faith [that he had in the first film] about whether or not he belongs in this family",[[31]](#cite_note-SGunnRocket2-33) with James Gunn adding, "this is really about Rocket coming to terms with accepting his place within a group of people, which probably seemed like a good idea" when they were heroes together at the end of the first film, but now "he's just not very comfortable with the idea". Feige stated that the relationship between Rocket and Groot has changed, saying, "Groot was Rocket's protector in the first movie, [and now] Rocket is Groot's protector."[[15]](#cite_note-ColliderFeb2017-17)
* [Michael Rooker](/wiki/Michael_Rooker "Michael Rooker") as [Yondu Udonta](/wiki/Yondu_Udonta_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Yondu Udonta (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A blue-skinned buccaneer of the Ravagers who is a fatherly figure to Quill and member of the Guardians. He is also the former deputy to Ayesha.[[32]](#cite_note-RookerConfirm-34)[[33]](#cite_note-GotG2ConceptArt2-35) Yondu has a larger head fin in the film, to look closer to his comic counterpart,[[34]](#cite_note-SDCC2016CBR-36) and Rooker worked with the prosthetic department to add gaps to his prosthetic teeth, "just like real teeth", to aid him in whistling as the character.[[35]](#cite_note-RookerGI-37) Rooker explained that, for the sequel, Gunn "wanted people to experience more in-depth what Yondu was thinking and how he's feeling—a more serious Yondu." Rooker noted the complex relationship between Yondu and Quill where "we don't agree on things...they are constantly at each other's throats [but] Yondu truly cares about this kid."[[36]](#cite_note-PressNotes-38) Gunn was reluctant to kill Yondu in the film, but ultimately felt that "this is a story about a father's love for his son, his ultimate love, so much love that he sacrifices himself for that, and that's what Yondu is. He is 100 percent Peter Quill's father" despite Ego being Quill's biological father.[[37]](#cite_note-YonduDeath-39) Before the release of *Vol. 2*, Rooker spent time on the set of *[Avengers: Infinity War](/wiki/Avengers:_Infinity_War "Avengers: Infinity War")* (2018) to counteract rumors that the reason his character would not appear in that film was that he would die in this one.[[38]](#cite_note-RookerInfinityWar-40)
* [Karen Gillan](/wiki/Karen_Gillan "Karen Gillan") as [Nebula](/wiki/Nebula_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Nebula (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  An adopted daughter of Thanos who was raised with Gamora as sisters and a former enemy of the Guardians.[[16]](#cite_note-NebulaGuardian-18) Gillan stated the film would further explore the sisterly relationship between Nebula and Gamora,[[39]](#cite_note-GillanNov2015-41) including their backstory "and what happened to these two girls growing up and actually how awful it was for them and how it has ruined their relationship",[[15]](#cite_note-ColliderFeb2017-17) adding "we're [also] going to start to see how much pain [Thanos] actually caused [Nebula]... we really start to see the emotional crack in her character".[[40]](#cite_note-GillanJan2017-42) While Gillan had to shave her head for the first film, she only had to shave half of her head for the sequel, taking away the underneath part and leaving the top.[[40]](#cite_note-GillanJan2017-42) Gillan's makeup took two and a half hours to apply, down from five hours for the first film.[[25]](#cite_note-SlashFilmFeb2017Pg3-27)
* [Pom Klementieff](/wiki/Pom_Klementieff "Pom Klementieff") as [Mantis](/wiki/Mantis_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Mantis (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A mantis-like creature and member of the Guardians with empathic powers who lives with Ego.[[15]](#cite_note-ColliderFeb2017-17)[[41]](#cite_note-MantisConfirm-43) Executive producer Jonathan Schwartz said the character "has never really experienced social interaction", and learns about "social intricacies" from the other Guardians.[[42]](#cite_note-GotG2ConceptArt-44) Klementieff added, "She was really lonely and by herself, so it's a completely new thing to meet these people and to discover new things", comparing this to a child making awkward mistakes in social situations. Mantis and Drax have an "interesting" relationship in the film due to both being "complete odd balls".[[15]](#cite_note-ColliderFeb2017-17) [Steve Englehart](/wiki/Steve_Englehart "Steve Englehart"), Mantis' co-creator, was disappointed with the character's portrayal, saying, "That character has nothing to do with Mantis ... I really don't know why you would take a character who is as distinctive as Mantis is and do a completely different character and still call her Mantis."[[43]](#cite_note-EnglehartMantis-45)
* [Sylvester Stallone](/wiki/Sylvester_Stallone "Sylvester Stallone") as [Stakar Ogord](/wiki/Stakar_Ogord_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Stakar Ogord (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  A high-ranking Ravager who holds a grudge against Yondu.[[44]](#cite_note-Stakar-46) Stallone likened his character's relationship with Yondu to a father-son relationship, and called the confrontation they have in the film "pretty intense".[[36]](#cite_note-PressNotes-38) For Stakar's acceptance of Yondu as a Ravager at the end of the film, Gunn asked Stallone to channel the "That'll do, pig" line from the film *[Babe](/wiki/Babe_(film) "Babe (film)")* (1995).[[45]](#cite_note-58ThingsCommentary2-47) Gunn described Stakar as "very important to the Marvel Universe",[[46]](#cite_note-AdamCarollaPodcast-48) and said that "it's our plan to see more of Stallone" in future MCU films, though he was not sure then if that would include *[Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._3 "Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3")* (2023).[[47]](#cite_note-OldTeam-49)
* [Kurt Russell](/wiki/Kurt_Russell "Kurt Russell") as [Ego](/wiki/Ego_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Ego (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"):  
  An ancient [Celestial](/wiki/Celestial_(comics) "Celestial (comics)") who is Quill's father.[[34]](#cite_note-SDCC2016CBR-36)[[48]](#cite_note-MarvelFilmingBegins-50) Russell portrays an avatar of Ego who is more traditionally seen in the comics in his "Living Planet" form. Pratt suggested Russell for the role,[[49]](#cite_note-EmpireAug2016-51) and Russell appreciated why he was wanted after seeing Pratt's performance in the first film, feeling, "That's my kinda guy. I know where that kind of goof comes from". He added, "I bring the right things [from previous roles]... I connected the dots from some of the things I've done in the past".[[50]](#cite_note-RussellSet-52) Pratt felt Quill would know what Kurt Russell looks like and should point out that Ego looks like Russell, but Gunn rejected the idea.[[51]](#cite_note-PrattsBadIdea-53) Ego replaces Quill's original comic book father [J'son](/wiki/J%27son_(character) "J'son (character)"),[[52]](#cite_note-EmpireAug2014-54) and was allowed to be used in the film after [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox") reached a deal with Marvel Studios to return Ego's film rights in exchange for changing the power set of [Negasonic Teenage Warhead](/wiki/Negasonic_Teenage_Warhead "Negasonic Teenage Warhead"), whom Fox wanted to use in *[Deadpool](/wiki/Deadpool_(film) "Deadpool (film)")* (2016).[[53]](#cite_note-EgoRights-55) Gunn originally thought Marvel held the rights to Ego and stated that, had the deal with Fox not been made, there was "no back up plan, and it would [have been] nearly impossible to just drop another character in" given the extensive work done surrounding the character.[[54]](#cite_note-GunnEgoRights-56) For the film's opening sequence, set in 1980 Missouri, [Aaron Schwartz](/wiki/Aaron_Schwartz_(American_actor) "Aaron Schwartz (American actor)") served as facial reference for the young Ego.[[55]](#cite_note-YoungEgo-57)[[56]](#cite_note-PressCredits-58)

[Elizabeth Debicki](/wiki/Elizabeth_Debicki "Elizabeth Debicki") appears as [Ayesha](/wiki/Ayesha_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Ayesha (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), the golden High Priestess and the leader of the Sovereign people,[[48]](#cite_note-MarvelFilmingBegins-50)[[57]](#cite_note-GotG2SDCC2016-59)[[58]](#cite_note-GunnSDCC2016Post-60) a genetically engineered race who are "gold and perfect and wanting to be physically and mentally impeccable". Gunn was "very specific" when writing the character, and after casting director Sarah Finn suggested Debicki, Gunn "knew right away that she was the one". Gunn highlighted the actress' beauty and 6 ft 3 in (1.91 m) height. Debicki wore platform shoes to increase her height to 6 ft 7 in (2.01 m).[[36]](#cite_note-PressNotes-38) [Chris Sullivan](/wiki/Chris_Sullivan_(actor) "Chris Sullivan (actor)") portrays [Taserface](/wiki/Taserface_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Taserface (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), the leader of a mutinous group of the Ravagers.[[48]](#cite_note-MarvelFilmingBegins-50)[[58]](#cite_note-GunnSDCC2016Post-60) Gunn originally posted a photo of the comic book character on his social media after landing *Guardians of the Galaxy*, calling him "the dumbest character of all time" and saying he would never feature the character in a film. After eventually deciding to use the character in the sequel, Gunn felt the character had given himself the name Taserface and is "a real dumbass".[[59]](#cite_note-Taserface-61) Sullivan's makeup took two-and-a-half to three-and-a-half hours to apply each day.[[60]](#cite_note-SullivanMakeup-62) [Sean Gunn](/wiki/Sean_Gunn "Sean Gunn") appears as [Kraglin Obfonteri](/wiki/Kraglin_Obfonteri "Kraglin Obfonteri"), Yondu's second-in-command in the Ravagers.[[61]](#cite_note-GillanSeanGunnConfirm-63) Kraglin has an expanded role compared to the previous film—Sean Gunn explained, "In the first film he did a lot of just saying yes and being at Yondu's side, but in this movie things take a little bit of a different turn as Kraglin has a little bit of a crisis of conscience and has to decide whether he's going to stick with Yondu or join forces with the growing faction of mutineers."[[36]](#cite_note-PressNotes-38)[[62]](#cite_note-SeanGunnDec2016-64)

Additionally, reprising their roles from the first film are [Laura Haddock](/wiki/Laura_Haddock "Laura Haddock") as [Meredith Quill](/wiki/Meredith_Quill_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Meredith Quill (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), [Gregg Henry](/wiki/Gregg_Henry "Gregg Henry") as her father [Jason](/wiki/Jason_Quill "Jason Quill"), [Seth Green](/wiki/Seth_Green "Seth Green") as the voice of [Howard the Duck](/wiki/Howard_the_Duck_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Howard the Duck (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), and canine actor Fred as [Cosmo](/wiki/Cosmo_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Cosmo (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[56]](#cite_note-PressCredits-58)[[63]](#cite_note-CreditCameos-65)[[64]](#cite_note-RTCameos-66) Members of Yondu's Ravager crew appearing in the film include [Evan Jones](/wiki/Evan_Jones_(actor) "Evan Jones (actor)") as Retch, [Jimmy Urine](/wiki/Jimmy_Urine "Jimmy Urine") as Half-Nut, [Stephen Blackehart](/wiki/Stephen_Blackehart "Stephen Blackehart") as Brahl,[[58]](#cite_note-GunnSDCC2016Post-60) [Steve Agee](/wiki/Steve_Agee "Steve Agee") as Gef,[[58]](#cite_note-GunnSDCC2016Post-60)[[65]](#cite_note-Agee-67) Mike Escamilla as Scrote,[[66]](#cite_note-Escamilla-68) Joe Fria as Oblo, Terence Rosemore as Narblik,[[58]](#cite_note-GunnSDCC2016Post-60) and [Tommy Flanagan](/wiki/Tommy_Flanagan_(actor) "Tommy Flanagan (actor)") as Tullk,[[58]](#cite_note-GunnSDCC2016Post-60)[[67]](#cite_note-Flanagan-69)[[68]](#cite_note-FlanaganRole-70) as well as [Charred Walls of the Damned](/wiki/Charred_Walls_of_the_Damned "Charred Walls of the Damned") drummer and *[Howard Stern Show](/wiki/Howard_Stern_Show "Howard Stern Show")* personality [Richard Christy](/wiki/Richard_Christy "Richard Christy") in a [cameo appearance](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance").[[69]](#cite_note-Christy-71) The other members of Stakar and Yondu's old team, based on the comic's [original incarnation of the Guardians of the Galaxy](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(1969_team) "Guardians of the Galaxy (1969 team)"), include [Michael Rosenbaum](/wiki/Michael_Rosenbaum "Michael Rosenbaum") as [Martinex](/wiki/Martinex_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Martinex (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), [Ving Rhames](/wiki/Ving_Rhames "Ving Rhames") as [Charlie-27](/wiki/Charlie-27_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Charlie-27 (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), and [Michelle Yeoh](/wiki/Michelle_Yeoh "Michelle Yeoh") as [Aleta Ogord](/wiki/Aleta_Ogord_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Aleta Ogord (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[47]](#cite_note-OldTeam-49)[[64]](#cite_note-RTCameos-66) Also included in the team are the CG characters [Krugarr](/wiki/Krugarr_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Krugarr (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") and [Mainframe](/wiki/Mainframe_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Mainframe (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), with the latter voiced, in an uncredited cameo, by [Miley Cyrus](/wiki/Miley_Cyrus "Miley Cyrus").[[47]](#cite_note-OldTeam-49)[[70]](#cite_note-Cyrus-72) Rosenbaum had previously auditioned to play Peter Quill in *Vol. 1*.[[71]](#cite_note-Rosenbaum-73) Gunn cast Yeoh because of his love of 1990s [Hong Kong films](/wiki/Cinema_of_Hong_Kong "Cinema of Hong Kong"),[[72]](#cite_note-OldTeam2-74) and Cyrus after admiring "the tone of her voice" while watching her as a coach on *[The Voice](/wiki/The_Voice_(American_TV_series) "The Voice (American TV series)")*.[[70]](#cite_note-Cyrus-72) He added that the team would return in future MCU films alongside Stallone's Stakar.[[47]](#cite_note-OldTeam-49)

[Stan Lee](/wiki/Stan_Lee "Stan Lee") appears as an informant to the [Watchers](/wiki/Watcher_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Watcher (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), discussing previous adventures that include his cameos in other MCU films; he specifically mentions his time as a [FedEx](/wiki/FedEx "FedEx") delivery man, which connects to Lee's cameo as a FedEx delivery man in *[Captain America: Civil War](/wiki/Captain_America:_Civil_War "Captain America: Civil War")* (2016).[[73]](#cite_note-LeeCameo3-75) This acknowledged the fan theory that Lee may be portraying the same character in all his cameos,[[74]](#cite_note-LeeCameo2-76) with Gunn noting that "people thought Stan Lee is a Watcher and that all of these cameos are part of him being a Watcher. So, Stan Lee as a guy who is working for the Watchers was something that I thought was fun for the MCU."[[73]](#cite_note-LeeCameo3-75)[[74]](#cite_note-LeeCameo2-76) Feige added that Lee "clearly exists, you know, above and apart from the reality of all the films. So the notion that he could be sitting there on a cosmic pit stop during the jump gate sequence in *Guardians*...really says, so wait a minute, he's this same character who's popped up in all these films?"[[75]](#cite_note-LeeCameo-77) Lee filmed several different versions of the scene, including an alternative where he references his role in *Deadpool*, which would have been the first acknowledgment of the [*X-Men* film series](/wiki/X-Men_(film_series) "X-Men (film series)") by an MCU film.[[74]](#cite_note-LeeCameo2-76) Gunn later admitted that the *Civil War* reference is a [continuity error](/wiki/Continuity_error "Continuity error"), given *Vol. 2* is set before the events of *Civil War*, saying, "I screwed up; I wasn't thinking. But I'm going to say that probably Stan Lee used the guise of a FedEx guy more than one time."[[73]](#cite_note-LeeCameo3-75)

[David Hasselhoff](/wiki/David_Hasselhoff "David Hasselhoff") makes a cameo appearance as himself, when Ego shape-shifts to his likeness,[[13]](#cite_note-ScreenRantCameos-15) while [Rob Zombie](/wiki/Rob_Zombie "Rob Zombie") once again has a Ravager voice cameo.[[56]](#cite_note-PressCredits-58)[[76]](#cite_note-ZombieCameo-78) Footage of [Jeff Goldblum](/wiki/Jeff_Goldblum "Jeff Goldblum") dancing as the [Grandmaster](/wiki/Grandmaster_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Grandmaster (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") from the set of *[Thor: Ragnarok](/wiki/Thor:_Ragnarok "Thor: Ragnarok")* (2017) is used briefly during the end credits, with Feige explaining that Marvel "thought it would be fun to put it in there" especially since the comic version of Grandmaster is the brother of the [Collector](/wiki/Collector_(character) "Collector (character)"), who appeared in the first *Guardians* film.[[77]](#cite_note-FeigeGrandmaster-79) [Ben Browder](/wiki/Ben_Browder "Ben Browder"), the star of the series *[Farscape](/wiki/Farscape "Farscape")*, of which Gunn is an avid fan, cameos as a Sovereign Admiral.[[78]](#cite_note-Browder-80) [Molly Quinn](/wiki/Molly_Quinn "Molly Quinn") appears as Howard the Duck's date.[[13]](#cite_note-ScreenRantCameos-15) Jim Gunn Sr. and Leota Gunn, parents of James and Sean Gunn, also make cameo appearances in the film as an elderly couple on Earth.[[79]](#cite_note-GunnNoClose-81) *[Jimmy Kimmel Live!](/wiki/Jimmy_Kimmel_Live! "Jimmy Kimmel Live!")*'s [Guillermo Rodriguez](/wiki/Guillermo_Rodriguez_(comedian) "Guillermo Rodriguez (comedian)") has an uncredited cameo appearance as a police officer on Earth when the seedling was xenoforming.[[80]](#cite_note-ScreenRant-82)[[81]](#cite_note-83)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

> "There are general ideas for what the sequel is and where it goes and who's involved and what happens and what we find out about our characters. So it's very general and that could change ... but I know a lot about who these characters are and where they came from and where they're going. I'm excited by the possibility of creating a sequel because we had to do a lot of setup in [the first] movie and with a sequel we don't have to do that setup which will make it so much easier for me."

—James Gunn, writer and director of *Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2*, on the state of the sequel in August 2014[[82]](#cite_note-SequelState-84)

In May 2014, [Disney](/wiki/Disney "Disney") CEO [Bob Iger](/wiki/Bob_Iger "Bob Iger") stated he felt the first *[Guardians of the Galaxy](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film) "Guardians of the Galaxy (film)")* film (2014) had "strong franchise potential".[[83]](#cite_note-Iger1-85) He added that their goal was to create "another *[Avengers](/wiki/The_Avengers_(2012_film) "The Avengers (2012 film)")* (2012)".[[84]](#cite_note-Iger2-86) [James Gunn](/wiki/James_Gunn "James Gunn"), who directed and co-wrote the first film, said that he would like to return for a sequel and that he was contractually obligated to if asked.[[85]](#cite_note-ColliderMay2014-87) The next month, producer [Kevin Feige](/wiki/Kevin_Feige "Kevin Feige") said there are "places we can take [the franchise] and we have ideas of where we'd like to go" based on the wide array of characters, worlds and storylines from the comics.[[86]](#cite_note-FeigeTotalFilm-88) In July 2014, *Guardians of the Galaxy* co-screenwriter [Nicole Perlman](/wiki/Nicole_Perlman "Nicole Perlman") confirmed a sequel, saying it was "going to happen" due to the positive internal response to the first film at Disney, and that Gunn would write and direct.[[87]](#cite_note-PerlmanSequel-89)[[88]](#cite_note-SequelVariety-90) At [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con") soon after, the sequel was given a release date of July 28, 2017.[[89]](#cite_note-GotG2Release-91)

Gunn had begun work on the film within a month, and said it would include at least one new [Guardian](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Guardians of the Galaxy (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") from the comics.[[90]](#cite_note-GunnNewGuardian-92)[[91]](#cite_note-SequelStart-93) In October, Gunn confirmed that all five original Guardians would return for the sequel, along with other supporting characters,[[92]](#cite_note-GunnVarityOct2014-94) and the release date was moved up to May 5, 2017.[[93]](#cite_note-Oct2014Event-95) In the following months, [Michael Rooker](/wiki/Michael_Rooker "Michael Rooker") said he would return as [Yondu Udonta](/wiki/Yondu_Udonta_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Yondu Udonta (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), and was looking forward to exploring new areas of the character.[[32]](#cite_note-RookerConfirm-34)[[94]](#cite_note-RookerGotG2-96) [Chris Pratt](/wiki/Chris_Pratt "Chris Pratt"), who played [Peter Quill/Star-Lord](/wiki/Peter_Quill_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Peter Quill (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") in the first film, confirmed the sequel would be one of his next projects.[[8]](#cite_note-PrattReturn-10) [Bradley Cooper](/wiki/Bradley_Cooper "Bradley Cooper") expressed interest and excitement when asked if he would return as [Rocket](/wiki/Rocket_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Rocket (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[95]](#cite_note-CooperReturn-97) [Vin Diesel](/wiki/Vin_Diesel "Vin Diesel") also confirmed he would return as [Groot](/wiki/Groot_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Groot (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[20]](#cite_note-DieselReturn-22) In March 2015, Gunn revealed that filming would take place in [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)") with "major crew changes" from the first film, and that his brother, [Sean Gunn](/wiki/Sean_Gunn "Sean Gunn"), would return as [Kraglin Obfonteri](/wiki/Kraglin_Obfonteri_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Kraglin Obfonteri (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") alongside [Karen Gillan](/wiki/Karen_Gillan "Karen Gillan") as [Nebula](/wiki/Nebula_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Nebula (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[61]](#cite_note-GillanSeanGunnConfirm-63)[[96]](#cite_note-GunnMar2015Q&A-98) He explained his relationship with [Marvel Studios](/wiki/Marvel_Studios "Marvel Studios"), saying the company "let me go and do my thing, and I truly listen to their notes and ideas. I've never been told to put in any character or plot element at all... When they trust you they give you a wide berth... we just fit".[[96]](#cite_note-GunnMar2015Q&A-98)

In May, Gunn said the sequel would feature fewer characters than the first film, and that he had planned to introduce two major new characters in the script—[Mantis](/wiki/Mantis_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Mantis (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") and [Adam Warlock](/wiki/Adam_Warlock_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Adam Warlock (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[5]](#cite_note-AdamWarlock-5)[[9]](#cite_note-ScreenRantJune2015-11) Talks had begun with an actor Gunn had in mind to portray Mantis,[[97]](#cite_note-GunnMay2015-99) while he had decided to remove Warlock due to the film "getting too busy", explaining, "one of the main things with *Guardians of the Galaxy* is not to add a bunch of characters, not to make it bigger in that way but to go deeper with the characters... and getting to know them more emotionally... everything is just getting too sprawling and too crazy for me in these superhero comic book movies".[[9]](#cite_note-ScreenRantJune2015-11) Gunn added, "I adored what we had done with him. I think we did something really creative and unique with Adam Warlock. But it was one character too many and I didn't want to lose Mantis and Mantis was more organically part of the movie anyway".[[5]](#cite_note-AdamWarlock-5) He noted that Warlock could appear in future Guardians films, and is considered "a pretty important part" of the cosmic side of the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") (MCU).[[5]](#cite_note-AdamWarlock-5)[[98]](#cite_note-SlashFilmFeb2017-100) The character's future introduction is hinted at in one of the film's [mid-credits scenes](/wiki/Post-credits_scene "Post-credits scene").[[4]](#cite_note-WarlockCredits-4) Gunn noted that Marvel gave him "some blowback" for including the Warlock teaser because they felt fans would believe the character would be appearing in *[Avengers: Infinity War](/wiki/Avengers:_Infinity_War "Avengers: Infinity War")* (2018) and *[Avengers: Endgame](/wiki/Avengers:_Endgame "Avengers: Endgame")* (2019), which was not the case. By including the scene, Gunn promised Marvel he would use his social media presence to clarify for fans that the character would not be seen in either of the [Avengers](/wiki/Avengers_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Avengers (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") films;[[99]](#cite_note-WarlockSceneBlowback-101) Gunn used the character in his sequel *[Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._3 "Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3")* (2023), but found it difficult to feature the character in that story, believing it was "kind of fitting a weird square peg into a round hole".[[100]](#cite_note-GunnEWJun2025-102)

#### Writing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Writing")]

After the film's announcement, Gunn said he knew "a lot of where I want to go [in the sequel]",[[101]](#cite_note-GunnFilmDivider-103) having written the backstory of Peter Quill, his father and his history with Yondu during the making of the first film with the intention of exploring them in a future film.[[21]](#cite_note-GunnSet-23) Gunn's earliest idea for a *Guardians* sequel involved Rocket and Groot starring as the main characters in a story fleshing out the former's [origin story](/wiki/Origin_story "Origin story"), but Gunn felt that he had to flesh out Peter Quill's origins first.[[102]](#cite_note-Rocket&GrootMovieCouldHaveReplaced-104) Gunn wanted to give the sequel a different structure from the first film, since "one of the reasons people like *Guardians* is because it's fresh and different, so the second one will be fresh and different from the first one".[[103]](#cite_note-VultureMusic-105) Before starting on the script, Gunn hoped to further explore [Drax](/wiki/Drax_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Drax (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), Nebula, Kraglin and [the Collector](/wiki/Collector_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Collector (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") and expand on [Xandarian](/wiki/Xandar "Xandar"), [Kree](/wiki/Kree "Kree"), [Krylorian](/wiki/List_of_alien_races_in_Marvel_Comics#K "List of alien races in Marvel Comics") and Ravager culture.[[101]](#cite_note-GunnFilmDivider-103)[[104]](#cite_note-GunnEWNov2014-106) He also hoped to introduce more female characters in the sequel, though wanted to avoid including "earthlings" such as [Carol Danvers](/wiki/Carol_Danvers_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Carol Danvers (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"),[[105]](#cite_note-ColliderFemaleCharacters-107) as well as Novas [Richard Rider](/wiki/Nova_(Richard_Rider) "Nova (Richard Rider)") or [Sam Alexander](/wiki/Nova_(Sam_Alexander) "Nova (Sam Alexander)"), saying, "I think Quill being the only earthling is important. That serves the entire movie-going audience and not just the handful of Nova [and Carol Danvers] fans".[[106]](#cite_note-GunnNova-108) The character [Darkhawk](/wiki/Darkhawk "Darkhawk") had also been considered for the film.[[107]](#cite_note-DarkhawkConsideration-109)

Feige said exploring Quill's father "would certainly be part of a next Guardians adventure", adding "I think there's a reason we seeded it at the very end of the [first] film like that".[[108]](#cite_note-FeigeQuillFather-110) Gunn also stated that he wanted to make sure "Yondu's place in everything made sense" in regards to his relationship to Quill and his father, and also revealed Quill's father would not be [J'son](/wiki/J%27son_(character) "J'son (character)") as in the comics.[[52]](#cite_note-EmpireAug2014-54) Gunn "was less confident [Marvel] was going to buy in on Baby Groot than" including Ego, since "adult Groot was the most popular character from the first film and I didn't think they'd want to risk a good thing". However, by changing Groot, Gunn felt it "opened the film up" creatively, allowing Gunn to bring out "new aspects of our other characters".[[109]](#cite_note-BabyGrootConfidence-111) Gunn said [Thanos](/wiki/Thanos_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Thanos (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") would only appear in the sequel "if he helps our story and he will not show up at all if not. Thanos is not the most important thing in *Guardians 2*, that's for damn sure. There's the Guardians themselves and other threats the Guardians are going to be facing that are not Thanos".[[52]](#cite_note-EmpireAug2014-54) Feige later confirmed that Thanos would not appear in the sequel, as he was being saved for a "grander" return.[[110]](#cite_note-FeigeThanos-112)[[111]](#cite_note-EmpireFeb2016-113) When asked about how the film would connect with the other [Phase Three](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_Three "Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase Three") films in the Marvel Cinematic Universe, Gunn said, "I don't feel beholden to that stuff at all. I think it's really about the Guardians and what they are doing".[[112]](#cite_note-GunnMTVNov2014-114)

Gunn set the film two to three months after the first film[[98]](#cite_note-SlashFilmFeb2017-100)[[113]](#cite_note-TimeFrame-115) "because he felt the group are just such fragile egos and he didn't think this story could start years later".[[98]](#cite_note-SlashFilmFeb2017-100) Major planets visited in the film include Sovereign, Berhart and Contraxia. Feige also stated two or three other worlds would be seen, as well as "a little bit of Earth in this film, but it's not these characters going to Earth".[[15]](#cite_note-ColliderFeb2017-17) In December 2014, Gunn revealed the story for the film was written, saying, "It's [still] constantly shifting, but I feel like it's pretty strong. I'm excited about it".[[114]](#cite_note-GunnCBRDec2014-116) By early February 2015, Gunn was "a few short weeks" away from submitting a full story treatment to Marvel, and said that when he first presented his idea for the sequel to the company they thought it was "risky". He described it as "not really based on anything" from the comics, being mainly an original story.[[115]](#cite_note-GunnApril2015-117)[[116]](#cite_note-GunnFeb2015-118) Gunn later referred to the full treatment as a "scriptment", "a 70-page combination of a script and a treatment and it goes through every beat of the movie".[[9]](#cite_note-ScreenRantJune2015-11) By April, he was preparing to write the screenplay,[[115]](#cite_note-GunnApril2015-117) and in May he was hoping to complete the script before he began work on *[The Belko Experiment](/wiki/The_Belko_Experiment "The Belko Experiment")* (2016) in June 2015.[[97]](#cite_note-GunnMay2015-99)

As he developed the story, Gunn considered to have [Gamora](/wiki/Gamora_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Gamora (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") killed off due to [Zoe Saldaña](/wiki/Zoe_Salda%C3%B1a "Zoe Saldaña")'s original expectations to only play the role in the first film.[[117]](#cite_note-ZoeWantedGamoraDeadEarlier-119) As he knew that Saldaña did not wish to play Gamora for a few years, Gunn considered to have Gamora sacrifice herself at the end of the film to save Peter so he could learn about himself, but Feige and [Louis D'Esposito](/wiki/Louis_D%27Esposito "Louis D'Esposito") talked Gunn out from taking that decision because it did not work well with the story, in addition to [their plans](/wiki/Production_of_Avengers:_Infinity_War_and_Avengers:_Endgame "Production of Avengers: Infinity War and Avengers: Endgame") to have Gamora die in *Infinity War* instead as the [Russo brothers](/wiki/Russo_brothers "Russo brothers") informed Gunn during months of planning *Vol. 2*.[[118]](#cite_note-JamesGunnsIdeaForGamorasDeath-120) After discussing it with Marvel and realizing that it would be better for Gamora to die in the third *[Avengers](/wiki/Avengers_(film_series) "Avengers (film series)")* film,[[119]](#cite_note-GunnTNYTInterviewMay2023-121) Gunn ultimately concluded that the story naturally progressed in a way that insisted that Yondu Udonta should be the one to die at the end to rescue Quill despite Gunn's initial reluctance in killing off Michael Rooker's character due to their friendship, but in retrospective, Gunn felt that the story worked out in a perfect way to set up *Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3*.[[118]](#cite_note-JamesGunnsIdeaForGamorasDeath-120)

### Pre-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Pre-production")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/James_Gunn_by_Gage_Skidmore_2.jpg/250px-James_Gunn_by_Gage_Skidmore_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Gunn_by_Gage_Skidmore_2.jpg)

Gunn promoting *Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2* at the 2016 San Diego Comic-Con

On June 2, 2015, Gunn announced on social media that he had completed the first draft of the screenplay, and that the film's title would not simply be *Guardians of the Galaxy 2*.[[120]](#cite_note-GunnJune2015-122) The same week, he confirmed the return of Saldaña, [Dave Bautista](/wiki/Dave_Bautista "Dave Bautista") and Cooper as Gamora, Drax and Rocket, respectively.[[9]](#cite_note-ScreenRantJune2015-11) At the end of the month, Gunn announced the film would be titled *Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2*,[[121]](#cite_note-VarietyVol2-123) saying he "came up with a LOT of titles for *Vol. 2*. But because 'Guardians of the Galaxy' is already so wordy, it seemed strange to add another bunch of words after it. I liked *Vol. 2* the best, so that's what I stuck on the cover of the screenplay—and, fortunately, [Marvel] liked it".[[122]](#cite_note-GunnTitleExplain-124)

In September 2015, Gunn said in a [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") post that he wanted to use [Sneepers](/wiki/List_of_alien_races_in_Marvel_Comics#S "List of alien races in Marvel Comics"), an alien race that first appeared in Marvel Comics in 1964, as background characters in the film, but was advised against it by Marvel's legal department because the name was too similar to [snípur](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sn%C3%ADpur "wikt:snípur"), the Icelandic word for [clitoris](/wiki/Clitoris "Clitoris").[[123]](#cite_note-Sneeper-125) The Marvel legal department later cleared the use of Sneepers in the film, in part because of all the media coverage Gunn's initial post received.[[124]](#cite_note-SneepersOk-126) At the end of the month, Feige stated casting announcements would be made before the end of 2015,[[125]](#cite_note-FeigeSep2015-127) and by the end of October, [Pom Klementieff](/wiki/Pom_Klementieff "Pom Klementieff") was cast as Mantis.[[41]](#cite_note-MantisConfirm-43)[[126]](#cite_note-Klementieff-128) Also, it was reported that [Matthew McConaughey](/wiki/Matthew_McConaughey "Matthew McConaughey") had recently turned down the role of "the villain" in the film,[[127]](#cite_note-McConaughey-129) in favor of *[The Dark Tower](/wiki/The_Dark_Tower_(2017_film) "The Dark Tower (2017 film)")* (2017). McConaughey felt he would have been "an amendment" in *Vol. 2* in "a colorful part [made] for another big-name actor".[[128]](#cite_note-McConaugheyPlayboy-130) In December, Gunn said that he was corresponding with [John C. Reilly](/wiki/John_C._Reilly "John C. Reilly") about him reprising his role as [Nova Corpsman](/wiki/Nova_Corps "Nova Corps") [Rhomann Dey](/wiki/Rhomann_Dey "Rhomann Dey"),[[129]](#cite_note-GunnDec2015-131) while [Kurt Russell](/wiki/Kurt_Russell "Kurt Russell") entered early talks to play Quill's father.[[130]](#cite_note-Russell-132) After the [death](/wiki/Death_of_David_Bowie "Death of David Bowie") of [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") in January 2016, Gunn said that there had been discussions for Bowie to appear in the film as a member of Yondu's original crew.[[131]](#cite_note-Bowie-133)[[132]](#cite_note-BowieRole-134) Gunn also stated that he had completed the final draft of the script.[[133]](#cite_note-FinalDraft-135) [Benicio del Toro](/wiki/Benicio_del_Toro "Benicio del Toro"), who portrayed the Collector in the first film, expressed interest in portraying the character again, despite Marvel not contacting him about the sequel;[[134]](#cite_note-DelToroNo-136) Gunn explained that the Collector "just didn't fit" into *Vol. 2*.[[135]](#cite_note-GunnApr2016-137) In early February, comedian [Steve Agee](/wiki/Steve_Agee "Steve Agee") was revealed to be in the film.[[65]](#cite_note-Agee-67)

[Prop master](/wiki/Property_master "Property master") Russell Bobbitt had difficulty finding the cassette decks that had been used in the first film, and all of the [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") [Walkman](/wiki/Walkman "Walkman") headsets they sourced for the sequel were broken. Sony also did not have any headsets available for filming, while three pairs Bobbitt found on [eBay](/wiki/EBay "EBay") cost around $1,800 and were not the exact model. Bobbitt eventually created six from scratch for *Vol. 2*. Other props he created for the film included two sets of blasters for Quill, with removable blaster cartridges, and "steampunk-looking weapons and belts" for the Ravagers;[[25]](#cite_note-SlashFilmFeb2017Pg3-27) Bobbitt explained that four different weapons were designed for the latter group, and then 15–20 versions of those were produced to be used by the various Ravager actors (there could be up to 85–95 Ravagers per scene). For their belts, the props team cut the leather themselves rather than buying existing belts, and then parts from different electronic devices such as radios and cell phones were glued together to make each belt "a unique piece of art". The prop department also made edible props for certain scenes: a prop of a [stinkbug](/wiki/Halyomorpha_halys "Halyomorpha halys")-inspired insect was made from chocolate and injected with black honey so it could be eaten on screen and "when he bit down the honey poured out of his mouth"; similarly, a "yarrow root" was designed based on enlarged images of pollen, and then created with non-dairy white chocolate to be eaten onscreen.[[36]](#cite_note-PressNotes-38)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Filming")]

Pre-shooting began on February 11, 2016,[[136]](#cite_note-GunnPreShoot-138) at [Pinewood Atlanta Studios](/wiki/Pinewood_Atlanta_Studios "Pinewood Atlanta Studios") in [Fayette County, Georgia](/wiki/Fayette_County,_Georgia "Fayette County, Georgia"),[[137]](#cite_note-SHHypeMar2015-139) under the working title *Level Up*,[[98]](#cite_note-SlashFilmFeb2017-100) with [Henry Braham](/wiki/Henry_Braham "Henry Braham") serving as [cinematographer](/wiki/Cinematographer "Cinematographer") and Scott Chambliss as [production designer](/wiki/Production_designer "Production designer").[[9]](#cite_note-ScreenRantJune2015-11)[[138]](#cite_note-Braham-140) Gunn noted that many of the crew from the first film, such as cinematographer [Ben Davis](/wiki/Ben_Davis_(cinematographer) "Ben Davis (cinematographer)") and production designer Charles Wood, signed on to work on *[Doctor Strange](/wiki/Doctor_Strange_(2016_film) "Doctor Strange (2016 film)")* (2016), and due to a late change in production schedule for that film, were unable to work on *Vol. 2*.[[139]](#cite_note-CrewChange-141)[[140]](#cite_note-VarietyGotGReview-142)

[Principal photography](/wiki/Principal_photography "Principal photography") began on February 17,[[48]](#cite_note-MarvelFilmingBegins-50) with Marvel confirming that Russell had joined the cast, and revealing that [Elizabeth Debicki](/wiki/Elizabeth_Debicki "Elizabeth Debicki") and [Chris Sullivan](/wiki/Chris_Sullivan_(actor) "Chris Sullivan (actor)") had also been cast, all in undisclosed roles.[[48]](#cite_note-MarvelFilmingBegins-50) The production used all 18 stages at Pinewood Atlanta Studios, an increase in stage space from what was used for the first film. Gunn said the sequel required more sets than the first and "our sets are very large, even though a lot of the film is CGI. I like to have as many practical sets as we can and make the environments as real as possible so it balances out the CGI elements". Despite this, Gunn noted that there were fewer locations featured in the sequel, with the focus instead on being more specific and detailed with fewer places shown. Sets constructed for the film included several for the Sovereign planet, for which Chambliss used a "1950s pulp fiction variation on 1930s art deco design aesthetic"; the Ravager's main ship in the film, the *[Eclector](/wiki/Eclector "Eclector")*, which was constructed in sections to provide a complete 360-degree view of the ship as well as the ability to move sections around and portray different areas of the ship; and the Iron Lotus establishment on the "pleasure planet" of Contraxia, which Chambliss wanted to feel like it had been put together from "a whole yard of repurposed junk where old spaceships are cast away and industrial materials that aren't of use anymore are just left to rot", creating a "kind of neon jungle in its own way and covered in ice and snow". Interiors for other ships were also constructed, to limit the amount of [blue screen](/wiki/Chroma_key "Chroma key") the actors had to interact with;[[36]](#cite_note-PressNotes-38) this includes the cockpit of Quill's ship that had been built for the first film before being stored in [London](/wiki/London "London") which was transported to Atlanta for the sequel.[[141]](#cite_note-BrahamFilmmaker-143) Originally shot was a scene featuring Star-Lord and Gamora kissing, establishing themselves as a couple, but Gunn ultimately cut the scene due to feeling that the kiss happened in a "weird time". He would later beg the *Infinity War* filmmakers to include the kiss there.[[119]](#cite_note-GunnTNYTInterviewMay2023-121)

*Vol. 2* was the first film to be shot with an [8K resolution](/wiki/8K_resolution "8K resolution") camera, the [Red](/wiki/Red_Digital_Cinema_Camera_Company "Red Digital Cinema Camera Company") Weapon 8K.[[142]](#cite_note-RedCamera-144) Braham had wanted to use a different camera than the [Alexa 65](/wiki/Arri_Alexa#Alexa_65 "Arri Alexa") that had been used for several other Marvel films, because he found it to be a "very big and heavy camera". He wanted a camera that could deliver equivalent image quality to the Alexa 65, and tested multiple options. He eventually met with Red employees, whom he had a positive experience with working on *[The Legend of Tarzan](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Tarzan_(film) "The Legend of Tarzan (film)")* (2016), and they introduced him to an early prototype for the Weapon 8K. He, Gunn, and Marvel decided to use the camera in September 2015, when only that single prototype existed, and spent three months working with Red to get the camera ready for filming.[[141]](#cite_note-BrahamFilmmaker-143) For the sequence where Rocket and Yondu escape from the Ravagers, a [Phantom Camera](/wiki/Phantom_Cam "Phantom Cam") was used to film scenes up to 2,000 frames per second, with the footage able to be moved from slow-motion to high-speed within a single shot. Each shot using the camera had to be carefully set up and choreographed.[[143]](#cite_note-VultureRavagersEscape-145) Additional scenes were also shot in [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") and its aspect ratio.[[98]](#cite_note-SlashFilmFeb2017-100) Braham filmed almost 85 percent of the film using a stabilizing technology he had contributed to called Stabileye, which he called "a [handheld](/wiki/Hand-held_camera "Hand-held camera") [dolly](/wiki/Tracking_shot "Tracking shot")" which allowed "a spontaneity to the way the camera moves that is different and that felt appropriate for this movie". For filming the cockpits of spaceships, Braham surrounded the practical sets with video panels and other light sources that he could use to create realistic lighting no matter the environment outside the ship. Exteriors of the ships were filmed with the camera on a [technocrane](/wiki/Technocrane "Technocrane"), but any shots inside the cockpits again used the Stabileye which is "so small that you can get it in there next to the actors".[[141]](#cite_note-BrahamFilmmaker-143)

In April 2016, Gunn revealed that Reilly would not be part of the film, and stated that there were many other characters he could not include in the film due to rights issues, saying that [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox") "owns so many awesome cosmic villains and minor characters that I'd love to play around with" such as [Annihilus](/wiki/Annihilus "Annihilus") and [Kang the Conqueror](/wiki/Kang_the_Conqueror "Kang the Conqueror").[[135]](#cite_note-GunnApr2016-137) Gunn also planned on filming scenes with [Glenn Close](/wiki/Glenn_Close "Glenn Close"), reprising her role as Nova Prime Irani Rael from the first film.[[21]](#cite_note-GunnSet-23): 4 When filming [Stan Lee](/wiki/Stan_Lee "Stan Lee")'s [cameo](/wiki/List_of_cameo_appearances_by_Stan_Lee "List of cameo appearances by Stan Lee"), Gunn also filmed two other [cameo appearances](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance") with Lee including one for *Doctor Strange*, to limit the amount of travel Lee had to do.[[79]](#cite_note-GunnNoClose-81)[[144]](#cite_note-GunnCameos-146) Additional filming for *Vol. 2* took place in [Cartersville, Georgia](/wiki/Cartersville,_Georgia "Cartersville, Georgia"),[[145]](#cite_note-Cartersville-147) [a state park](/wiki/List_of_Oregon_state_parks "List of Oregon state parks") north of [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"),[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31) and in [St. Charles, Missouri](/wiki/St._Charles,_Missouri "St. Charles, Missouri").[[146]](#cite_note-58ThingsCommentary-148) The [Georgia International Convention Center](/wiki/Georgia_International_Convention_Center "Georgia International Convention Center") served as additional soundstage space after [pre-production](/wiki/Pre-production "Pre-production") on *[Spider-Man: Homecoming](/wiki/Spider-Man:_Homecoming "Spider-Man: Homecoming")* (2017) began at several Pinewood Atlanta Studios soundstages.[[98]](#cite_note-SlashFilmFeb2017-100) Principal photography [wrapped](/wiki/Wrap_(filmmaking) "Wrap (filmmaking)") on June 16, 2016.[[147]](#cite_note-FilmingEnd-149)

### Post-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Post-production")]

At San Diego Comic-Con in 2016, Russell and Debicki were revealed to be playing Ego, Quill's father, and Ayesha, respectively.[[34]](#cite_note-SDCC2016CBR-36) [Sylvester Stallone](/wiki/Sylvester_Stallone "Sylvester Stallone") was also revealed to be in the film,[[34]](#cite_note-SDCC2016CBR-36) with his role later revealed as [Stakar Ogord](/wiki/Starhawk_(character) "Starhawk (character)").[[44]](#cite_note-Stakar-46) Gunn also introduced multiple actors who were playing Ravager characters, since the Ravagers have a larger presence in the film.[[58]](#cite_note-GunnSDCC2016Post-60) On the decision to reveal Russell as Ego and Quill's father when he did, Gunn felt that since "people were going to figure it out eventually [...] it was better that we took the reins in our own hands" and make the reveal. Gunn also felt that this would move the focus of audiences from wanting to discover who Quill's father is to "the story and the relationship that these characters have".[[148]](#cite_note-GunnEgoReveal-150) In August 2016, Gunn confirmed the film would feature a [post-credits scene](/wiki/Post-credits_scene "Post-credits scene"),[[113]](#cite_note-TimeFrame-115) later stating there would be five in total, with four mid-credits scenes and one post-credits.[[149]](#cite_note-5PostCreditScenes-151)[[150]](#cite_note-PostCreditsScenes-152) Gunn also planned a sixth scene, where the Ravager Gef is found "mortally wounded" on the ship, but "it ended up being a little confusing".[[151]](#cite_note-Gunn6thPostCredit-153)

In November, Gillan revealed that the film was undergoing [reshoots](/wiki/Pick-up_(filmmaking) "Pick-up (filmmaking)").[[152]](#cite_note-GillanReshoots-154) That February, it was reported that the film had scored a perfect 100 in [test screenings](/wiki/Test_screening "Test screening"), the highest for any Marvel Studios film. *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* noted this score could not necessarily be compared to any non-Marvel movies due to Marvel's specific testing process that selects its audience "from a more select pool of recruits, what it terms 'friends and families screenings'".[[153]](#cite_note-PerfectTestScreening-155) In March 2017, Gunn revealed that [Michael Rosenbaum](/wiki/Michael_Rosenbaum "Michael Rosenbaum") would appear in the film,[[46]](#cite_note-AdamCarollaPodcast-48) and that Gunn would provide reference for Baby Groot's dancing "in a much bigger way [than the first film]. I actually had to do like a full day's worth of dancing to get Groot's dance down this time. Last time it was me in front of an [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone"), and this time it's me dancing on a huge soundstage and shooting it from five different angles".[[154]](#cite_note-GunnGrootDancing-156) [Fred Raskin](/wiki/Fred_Raskin "Fred Raskin") and [Craig Wood](/wiki/Craig_Wood_(film_editor) "Craig Wood (film editor)") returned from the first film to serve as editors.[[48]](#cite_note-MarvelFilmingBegins-50) On scenes that were cut from the film, Gunn said [Nathan Fillion](/wiki/Nathan_Fillion "Nathan Fillion"), who had had a voice cameo in the first film, was going to appear as [Simon Williams](/wiki/Wonder_Man "Wonder Man") in a sequence that would have shown several [movie posters](/wiki/Movie_posters "Movie posters") for films starring Williams, including films in which he portrays [Arkon](/wiki/Arkon "Arkon") and [Tony Stark](/wiki/Tony_Stark_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Tony Stark (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[155]](#cite_note-Fillion-157)[[156]](#cite_note-FillionLeak-158) Gunn chose Williams for Fillion's cameo because he wanted Fillion to have a bigger role in the MCU at some point and so did not want to give him a role that could prevent him from taking a more substantial one later. Gunn still considered Fillion's cameo canon to the MCU, despite it being cut.[[157]](#cite_note-Fillion2-159) He also confirmed that Close's scenes had been cut from the film, because it felt like he "was trying to cram Nova Prime into the second movie as opposed to having it happen organically".[[79]](#cite_note-GunnNoClose-81)

#### Visual effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Visual effects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/ac/Ego%27s_planet_in_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2.jpg/250px-Ego%27s_planet_in_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ego%27s_planet_in_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Apollonian_gasket.svg/250px-Apollonian_gasket.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Apollonian_gasket.svg)

Ego's planet (top) was designed to be "highly geometric" and made extensive use of [fractals](/wiki/Fractal "Fractal"), including [Apollonian gaskets](/wiki/Apollonian_gasket "Apollonian gasket") (bottom).[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31)

Visual effects for the film were created by [Framestore](/wiki/Framestore "Framestore"), [Weta Digital](/wiki/Weta_Digital "Weta Digital"), Trixter, [Method Studios](/wiki/Method_Studios "Method Studios"), [Animal Logic](/wiki/Animal_Logic "Animal Logic"), [Scanline VFX](/wiki/Scanline_VFX "Scanline VFX"), Lola VFX, Luma, and [Cantina Creative](/wiki/Cantina_Creative "Cantina Creative").[[158]](#cite_note-GunnVFXVendors-160) Previsualization was done by The Third Floor.[[159]](#cite_note-OpeningSequence-161) Framestore created 620 shots for the film, Weta Digital created 530, Method Studios more than 500, and Animal Logic created 147.[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31) Framestore once again created Rocket and Groot,[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31)[[160]](#cite_note-THRVFX-162) with Method Studios, Weta Digital and Trixter also working on Rocket. Framestore rebuilt Rocket "from the ground up" for *Vol. 2*, giving him an updated fur simulation, new facial shapes and phonemes, as well as a new eye rig, which came from a rig used for the character Gnarlack from *[Fantastic Beasts and Where to Find Them](/wiki/Fantastic_Beasts_and_Where_to_Find_Them_(film) "Fantastic Beasts and Where to Find Them (film)")* (2016).[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31) Trixter's work on Rocket included when he sets off a trap for the Ravagers, additionally working on the sequence when Rocket, Yondu and Kraglin jump across space.[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31) Other work from Framestore included creatures, spaceships,[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31) Ayesha's lair, the space chase, the *Eclector* scenes, and the adolescent Groot post-credit scene.[[161]](#cite_note-TitleSequence-163)

Framestore also created the film's opening sequence.[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31) Despite appearing as a single shot, it ultimately was composed of 11 different visual effect shots. Gunn provided reference video for Baby Groot's dancing in the sequence.[[159]](#cite_note-OpeningSequence-161) Framestore animation supervisor Arslan Elver noted that the majority of the opening sequence includes CGI and digital doubles, with three moments (Quill falling on the ground near Baby Groot, Gamora talking to Baby Groot, and Drax rolling behind Baby Groot) consisting of actual photography.[[161]](#cite_note-TitleSequence-163) As with the first film, Sarofsky created the typography for the opening, using the same style from *Guardians of the Galaxy* for consistency, "this time in rusty gold and glowing blue neon". Executive Creative Director Erin Sarofsky noted the challenge of placing the credits while the sequence was being refined by Framestore. Sarofsky proposed several different options for the credits, including having "a little hovering character that projected a hologram up on the screen", but Gunn did not want another character in the sequence to deal with. A simpler 2D option was used instead. Sarofsky also worked on the end credits, the first time for the company, integrating the actual credits from company [Exceptional Minds](/wiki/Exceptional_Minds "Exceptional Minds") with designs inspired by old music album art, taking albums and scanning them for their old textures and scratches.[[161]](#cite_note-TitleSequence-163)

The destruction of the Ravager ship the *Eclector* was handled by Weta Digital.[[162]](#cite_note-WetaEclector-164) Weta Digital also handled Ego during his fight with Quill, utilizing a digital double of Russell for many of their shots. Weta also needed to create a digital double for [David Hasselhoff](/wiki/David_Hasselhoff "David Hasselhoff") for the moment when Ego shifts into Hasselhoff's guise. Guy Williams, Weta's visual effects supervisor, said, "We tried morphing to a live-action Hoff—but it did not hold up as well. The Kurt version looked better than the Hoff version ... and while we had built a very detailed Kurt digi-double ... we didn't want to go to the same level on the Hoff for just two shots. But in the end we did have to do a partial build digi-double of the Hoff. The reason we did the Kurt digi-double in the first place was to make sure all the effects stuck correctly to the body ... so we went with the approach of a full digi-double, the hair, the side of the face everything on Kurt. For the Hoff, we got pretty close, but it is not quite as detailed as for Kurt".[[163]](#cite_note-FXGuide1-165) Additional work by Weta included the inside of Ego's planet, known as the Planet Hollow, which was inspired by the [fractal art](/wiki/Fractal_art "Fractal art") of Hal Tenny, who Gunn hired to help design Ego's environment.[[160]](#cite_note-THRVFX-162)[[164]](#cite_note-EmpireMar2017-166) Gunn added that there are "over a trillion polygons on Ego's planet," calling it "the biggest visual effect of all time. There's nothing even close to it".[[164]](#cite_note-EmpireMar2017-166)

Animal Logic and Method Studios also worked on the various parts of Ego's planet, with Animal Logic focusing on Ego's cathedral, and Method on the arrival sequence and Baby Groot's "not that button" sequence in the Planet Hollow. Animal Logic's work for Ego's cathedral was also based on fractal art. The team at Animal Logic were initially brought on to the film to work on the story vignettes that were used to explain the backstory, which started as oil paintings before evolving to falling sand, and eventually the final plastic sculptures that were used. Method also created the film's final sequence for Yondu's funeral. To make Rocket shedding a tear convincing, Method used in-house footage of Animation Supervisor Keith Roberts "performing the scene for reference, studying the macro facial movements like minor eye darts or blinks, in addition to what was filmed on set and in the sound booth by" Cooper. Additional work included the scenes on the planet Berhart.[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31) Lola VFX worked on [de-aging](/wiki/De-aging_in_film "De-aging in film") Russell, having previously done similar work in other MCU films; they also added to various characters, including Nebula. To achieve the younger Ego, Lola referenced Russell's performance in *[Used Cars](/wiki/Used_Cars "Used Cars")* (1980), as "he had a lot of the [facial] action" the visual effects artists were looking for. They also used a younger stand in, [Aaron Schwartz](/wiki/Aaron_Schwartz_(American_actor) "Aaron Schwartz (American actor)"), since he had "big broad jaw, chin, and most importantly the way the laugh lines move[d] and crease[d] as he talk[ed]", similar to Russell's.[[163]](#cite_note-FXGuide1-165) [Laura Haddock](/wiki/Laura_Haddock "Laura Haddock"), reprising her role from the first film of Meredith Quill in the sequence, was also slightly de-aged to play the younger version of that character.[[146]](#cite_note-58ThingsCommentary-148) Luma worked on the Sovereign people and their world, and Adam Warlock's cocoon.[[29]](#cite_note-FXGuidePart2-31)

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [*Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2* (soundtrack)](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2_(soundtrack) "Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2 (soundtrack)")

By August 2014, Gunn had "some ideas listed, but nothing for sure" in terms of songs to include in Quill's *Awesome Mix Vol. 2* mixtape,[[103]](#cite_note-VultureMusic-105) for which he felt "a little pressure" due to the positive response to [the first film's soundtrack](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(soundtrack)#Guardians_of_the_Galaxy:_Awesome_Mix_Vol._1_(Original_Motion_Picture_Soundtrack) "Guardians of the Galaxy (soundtrack)"). Gunn added, "But I feel like the soundtrack in the second one is better."[[165]](#cite_note-SoundtrackFeelings-167) By June 2015, Gunn had chosen all of the songs and built them into the script,[[9]](#cite_note-ScreenRantJune2015-11) calling *Awesome Mix Vol. 2* "more diverse" than the first one, with "some really incredibly famous songs and then some songs that people have never heard."[[166]](#cite_note-GunnSoundtrackDec2016-168) [Tyler Bates](/wiki/Tyler_Bates "Tyler Bates") was confirmed to score the film by August 2015, returning from the first film.[[167]](#cite_note-Bates-169) As with *Guardians of the Galaxy*, Bates wrote some of the score first so Gunn could film to the music, as opposed to Bates scoring to the film.[[129]](#cite_note-GunnDec2015-131) Recording for the score occurred in January 2017 at [Abbey Road Studios](/wiki/Abbey_Road_Studios "Abbey Road Studios").[[168]](#cite_note-GunnScoreJan-170) *Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2: Awesome Mix Volume 2*, along with the film score album composed by Bates, were released on April 21, 2017.[[169]](#cite_note-AwesomeMixVol2ReleaseDate-171)[[170]](#cite_note-ScoreReleaseDate-172) A cassette version of *Awesome Mix Volume 2* was released on June 23, 2017, while a deluxe-edition vinyl LP featuring both *Awesome Mix Volume 2* and Bates' score was released on August 11, 2017.[[171]](#cite_note-CassetteRelease-173)

## Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Marketing")]

In June 2016, Marvel announced plans for an expanded merchandising program for the sequel, with Groot playing a central role.[[172]](#cite_note-GotG2Merch-174) Gunn ensured the film's female characters received more representation in the merchandise than in the first.[[173]](#cite_note-GunnFemaleMerchandise-175) Paul Gitter, senior vice president of licensing at [Disney Consumer Products](/wiki/Disney_Consumer_Products "Disney Consumer Products"), said they intended to build *Guardians of the Galaxy* into a tentpole franchise. Partners in the campaign included [Hasbro](/wiki/Hasbro "Hasbro"), [Lego](/wiki/Lego "Lego"), [Funko](/wiki/Funko "Funko"), [LB Kids](/wiki/LB_Kids "LB Kids"),[[172]](#cite_note-GotG2Merch-174) [GEICO](/wiki/GEICO "GEICO"), [Ford Motor Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company"), [Go-Gurt](/wiki/Go-Gurt "Go-Gurt"), [Hanes](/wiki/Hanes "Hanes"), [Synchrony Bank](/wiki/Synchrony_Bank "Synchrony Bank"), [Dairy Queen](/wiki/Dairy_Queen "Dairy Queen"), [M&M's](/wiki/M%26M%27s "M&M's"), [Screenvision](/wiki/Screenvision "Screenvision"), [Sprint Corporation](/wiki/Sprint_Corporation "Sprint Corporation"), and [Wrigley Company](/wiki/Wrigley_Company "Wrigley Company").[[174]](#cite_note-DeadlineMarketing-176) Additionally, Marvel partnered with [Doritos](/wiki/Doritos "Doritos") for their Rock Out Loud campaign to create a custom "limited-edition series of Doritos bags featuring a built-in cassette tape deck-inspired player that plays" *Awesome Mix Vol. 2* and can be recharged.
